text: The surgeon begins the procedure by mobilizing the left colon, taking care to preserve the layer of the superior hypogastric plexus during dissection.

The surgeon precisely separates the avascular layer between the mesocolon and the retroperitoneum. High ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery is performed, followed by ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein at the same level.

The surgeon further mobilizes the left mesocolon, identifying and preserving the hypogastric nerves behind the rectosigmoid. The prehypogastric nerve fascia is incised and opened between the hypogastric nerves to access the presacral space. Sharp dissection is used to dissect the vascular presacral space as caudally as possible. The lateral attachment of the left colon is dissected, fully mobilizing the left colon up to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon performs nerve-preserving pelvic dissection, carefully preserving the whitish connective tissue on the autonomic nerves to prevent injury. Sharp dissection is executed precisely between the yellowish mesocolon and the whitish connective tissue, with counter traction aiding in the dissection process.

The surgeon dissects the end-pelvic fascia, exposing the surface of the levator muscle. On the anterior side, the whitish connective tissue on the seminal vesicle is preserved. Behind the seminal vesicle, the neurovascular bundle is identified and carefully preserved with its covering connective tissue. As the dissection progresses distally, the neurovascular bundle is seen approaching medially.

The surgeon exposes the prostate and begins intersphincteric dissection between the pubococcygeal muscle and the rectal wall. The rectococcygeal muscle is divided posterior to the rectal wall, with careful attention to avoid injury. The puborectal muscle is located within the anal canal, inside the pubococcygeal muscle sling, and the dissection proceeds to separate the intersphincteric plane. The anterior view shows the neurovascular bundle completely preserved.

The recto-urethral muscle is continuous from the rectal muscle and attaches anteriorly behind the distal prostate. The puborectal muscle is located inside of the pubococcygeal muscle. Intersphincteric plane is located inside the puborectal muscle. This anatomical understanding is very important for precise intersphincteric dissection. If you go outside, you may injure the external sphincter and cause severe incontinence.

The surgeon transitions to an anal approach, closing the rectal lumen with purse string sutures. The mucosa and internal sphincter muscle are incised just above the dentate line, allowing entry into the previously dissected intersphincteric plane. Anteriorly, the pink recto-urethral muscle is visible, with the whitish perineal body located behind it.
The surgeon retrieves the resected specimen from the anus. The anastomosis is then completed using sixteen vertical mattress sutures.
